column8: Builders Club (BC) was the first paid membership system introduced on Roblox in 2007. It provided users with exclusive benefits such as a daily Robux stipend, increased group capacity, and the ability to create and sell clothing items. Membership also granted exclusive badges and previously enabled features such as personal servers and MEGA places.
column9: Roblox; Builders Club; Membership System; Virtual Economy; Old Roblox; Digital Culture

column8: Turbo Builders Club (TBC) was a mid-tier Roblox membership introduced in 2009 as an upgrade above Classic Builders Club. It provided increased daily Robux payouts, expanded group limits, and exclusive membership rewards such as a Turbo Builders Club badge and hard hat. While more expensive than Classic Builders Club, it did not include the highest-tier privileges of Outrageous Builders Club, such as Developer Exchange eligibility.
column9: Roblox; Turbo Builders Club; Membership System; Virtual Economy; Old Roblox; Digital Status System
column18: Screenshot from public forum use for educational and archival purposes

column8: Outrageous Builders Club (OBC) was the highest tier of the Builders Club membership system introduced in 2009. It provided users with the most extensive benefits, including the highest daily Robux stipend, expanded group limits, and access to Developer Exchange (DevEx) for eligible users. OBC members also received exclusive profile badges and platform recognition as high-status users. This tier became especially associated with experienced developers and administrators due to its monetization and creation privileges.

column8: Classic Avatar Culture describes an early Roblox identity system shaped by technical limitations rather than cosmetic choice. Avatars typically consisted of blocky default body parts, minimal accessories, and limited clothing systems, where hats and body colors carried most of the visual identity. Before modern shirt and pants systems were fully developed, players often improvised outfits using solid body colors and classic T-shirts to simulate clothing. This created a constraint-driven identity style where expression was emergent rather than designed. In modern Roblox culture, “Classic Avatars” are also recreated nostalgically in themed games, representing early-era aesthetics and design limitations.
column9: Roblox; Avatar Culture; Identity Systems; Classic Era Roblox; Nostalgia Avatars; Early Roblox Design; User-Generated Identity
column18: Screenshot from public forum use for educational and archival purposes

column8: This artifact represents an early Roblox Catalog webpage interface during the Ticket (Tix) and Builders Club era. The page includes a mixed content environment featuring Roblox catalog items, third-party advertisements (Google Ads, including unrelated retail promotions such as Circuit City), and early UI navigation elements such as “My ROBLOX,” “Games,” “Catalog,” “Builders Club,” “Forum,” and “News.” The catalog displays early cosmetic hats and items (e.g., ROBLOX Visor, Santa Hat, Firefighter Helmet, Floppy Fish) priced in Tickets (Tix), illustrating the pre-Robux economy system. The interface also shows early moderation settings (“Age: 13+, Chat Mode: Safe”), reflecting early safety segmentation systems. This snapshot demonstrates the hybrid nature of early Roblox as both a game platform and an ad-supported browser-based virtual world, where external advertising coexisted directly alongside user-generated item economies.
